title: AP World History: Modern SAQ Practice Exam 03

questions.prompt:
Source 1: Historian Priya Raman, “Rule and Adaptation in the Delhi Sultanate,” modern historical interpretation, 2020.
“The Delhi Sultanate’s rulers did not simply impose a foreign political order on northern India. Their power depended on adaptation: using Persianate court culture, Islamic legal authority, military elites, and practical arrangements with local Hindu landholders and administrators.”
Source 2: Historian Marcus Bell, “Military States of Medieval Eurasia,” modern historical interpretation, 2017.
“The Delhi Sultanate should be understood primarily as a military state. Its rulers survived because they controlled cavalry forces, fortified cities, tax collection, and campaigns against rivals. Cultural accommodation mattered, but coercive power was the foundation of the sultanate’s authority.”
(a) Identify one difference between the two historians’ interpretations of the Delhi Sultanate.
(b) Describe one specific historical development that could be used to support one of the interpretations.
(c) Explain one way the Delhi Sultanate was similar to or different from another state during c. 1200–1450.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a clear difference between the interpretations, such as Source 1 emphasizing adaptation and cooperation with local elites while Source 2 emphasizes military power, taxation, and coercion.
(b) 1 point: Describes a relevant development, such as the use of Persianate administration, Islamic legitimacy, military slavery or cavalry forces, iqta-style revenue assignments, fortified cities, cooperation with Hindu landholders, or campaigns against rival kingdoms.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid similarity or difference with another state, such as the Mongol Khanates, Mali, the Mamluk Sultanate, Song China, or states in Southeast Asia, using specific evidence about military power, religion, bureaucracy, taxation, or elite cooperation.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should avoid describing the Delhi Sultanate as either only a religious state or only a military conquest regime. Strong responses should show how political authority could depend on both force and adaptation. For part (c), the best answers name another state and compare a specific method of rule rather than making a vague statement about “strong governments.”

questions.prompt:
Source: Letter from a Mughal court official to a provincial revenue administrator, Agra, 1583. This excerpt is original but modeled on the style of Mughal administrative correspondence during Akbar’s reign.
“The emperor desires that assessment be made with fairness so that the cultivator may return to his field without fear. In districts where Rajput chiefs have entered imperial service, their honors must be respected, provided that they collect revenue according to regulation and provide soldiers when required. Let no official disturb temples, mosques, or shrines without cause, for disorder in matters of faith harms the peace of the realm. The strength of the empire lies not only in conquest but in making many peoples useful to one order.”
(a) Identify the likely purpose of the letter.
(b) Explain how the letter reflects one method used by the Mughal Empire to govern a diverse population.
(c) Explain one limitation of the letter as evidence for the experiences of ordinary people under Mughal rule.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a plausible purpose, such as instructing a provincial official to collect revenue fairly, maintain order, cooperate with Rajput elites, respect local religious institutions, or strengthen imperial authority.
(b) 1 point: Explains a relevant Mughal method of rule, such as incorporating local elites, using revenue administration, practicing religious accommodation under Akbar, maintaining military obligations, or balancing conquest with negotiated loyalty.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid limitation, such as the letter representing the perspective of imperial officials rather than peasants, women, artisans, religious minorities, or rebellious groups; or noting that official policy may not reflect actual local conditions.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong response should use the source’s perspective as an imperial administrative document. The letter presents Mughal rule as orderly and inclusive because it is written by an official trying to guide provincial governance. Strong answers should connect the source to broader Mughal strategies without assuming that official ideals always matched the lived experiences of ordinary people.

questions.prompt:
Source: Description of a Japanese woodblock print produced in Tokyo, c. 1875.
The print shows a newly built schoolroom with glass windows, desks, a wall clock, and a large map of the world. Boys and girls sit in rows while a teacher points to a chart showing steamships, telegraph wires, and a railway engine. Outside the window, a uniformed official stands near a street with gas lamps and Western-style brick buildings. A caption reads: “Learning for a Strong Country.”
(a) Identify one reform goal suggested by the described print.
(b) Explain one way the Meiji Restoration contributed to the reform goal shown in the print.
(c) Explain one way Japanese reform efforts during the late nineteenth century were similar to or different from reform efforts in another state during c. 1750–1900.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 3
(a) 1 point: Identifies a valid reform goal, such as expanding modern education, strengthening the state, promoting industrialization, adopting Western technology, training citizens, or building national power.
(b) 1 point: Explains how the Meiji Restoration contributed to reform, such as centralizing authority, ending many samurai privileges, promoting state-led industrialization, building railways, adopting Western military and educational models, or revising institutions to resist imperial domination.
(c) 1 point: Explains a valid similarity or difference with another reforming state, such as the Ottoman Empire, Qing China, Russia, or Egypt, using specific evidence about military reform, industrialization, education, constitutionalism, state centralization, or responses to foreign pressure.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong answer should interpret the visual details as evidence of state-directed modernization. The schoolroom, world map, technology chart, official, and slogan all point to the link between education, technology, and national strength. Strong comparisons should explain why reform programs emerged and how they differed in success, scope, or political context.
